text:
In the fall of 2010, we measured simultaneously hygroscopic growth of particles at 90% RH, cloud condensation nuclei (CCN) activity and size resolved composition in the Thüringer Wald mountains, central Germany. The hygroscopic growth factor for particles ranging in diameter from 50 to 250 nm increased from a median value of about 1.38 to one of about 1.28.

Closure between measured and modelled hygroscopicity Our measurements of particle hygroscopic growth (diameter increase as a function of ambient water vapour, Δd) were used in a closure analysis with derived κ values (κchem) using the Zdanovskii–Stokes–Robinson (ZSR) model. Use of the mean chemical composition (size averaged) for all particles resulted in an overestimation of their κ values (by up to 30–40% for 150 and 100 nm particles). However, by using the individual size resolved composition data improved closure was achieved. We propose that the major factor affecting this discrepancy is the evaporation of semivolatile $NH_4NO_3$ within the instrument.

These data are used to investigate relationships between the hygroscopicities of organic compounds and the oxygen to carbon ratio, and to evaluate models of organic water uptake in relation to droplet activation. We derive a regression relating the hygroscopicity of the organic fraction, κ org , to the oxygen to carbon ratio of the organic compounds in the aerosol and use it to constrain this parameter in closure models. Relationships between the measured κ values derived from CCN and subsaturated HTDMA experiments are also investigated. Measurements indicate that κ values derived from CCN measurements are generally 30% larger than those derived from corresponding sub-saturated HTDMA measurements, indicating greater influence from surface tension decrease, solution non-ideality, and condensation of semivolatile precursors upon activation. While we are able to accurately reproduce measured κ CCN using size-resolved information and a ZSR parameterization, we find that corresponding use of sub-saturated hygroscopicity data yields less accurate representations of the corresponding κ CCN measurements.
